mnemonics: DIAMETER: Distance In A Metric, Extremes Recorded - the greatest distance between any two points in the set.
keyPoints: The diameter of a set A in a metric space (X,d) is defined as diam(A) = sup{d(x,y) : x,y ∈ A}.
A set is bounded if and only if its diameter is finite.
The diameter of a non-empty set is always non-negative, and the diameter of the empty set is defined to be 0.
commonMistakes: Assuming the supremum (least upper bound) is always achieved by two specific points. Some sets (like open balls) may not contain the two points that are exactly the diameter apart.
Confusing diameter with radius. The diameter is the greatest distance between two points in the set, while the radius (in the context of a ball) is the distance from the center to the boundary.
